summary: Gruber offers a comprehensive reaction to Apple's September 2016 event unveiling the iPhone 7, Apple Watch Series 2, and several surprises. He highlights Nintendo's Super Mario Run announcement as a vindication of his years-long argument that Nintendo should make original iOS games. He defends the iPhone 7 against critics dismissing it for looking like the 6S, arguing that internal improvements matter far more than cosmetic redesign. The Apple Watch discussion focuses on the pivot toward fitness, the smart rebranding that eliminated 'Sport,' and the promising Nike and Hermès partnerships. He notes the ceramic Edition as a correction from the original gold pricing misstep, and praises AirPods while noting Apple strategically avoided saying 'Bluetooth' on stage.
key_points: Nintendo's Super Mario Run validates Gruber's three-year-old prediction that Nintendo should make original iOS games with premium pricing rather than free-to-play exploitation

Critics dismissing the iPhone 7 for looking like the 6S are missing the point — judging phones by external design changes rather than meaningful internal improvements is like reviewing a Porsche 911 without driving it
Apple Watch Series 2's fitness focus reflects Apple's belated understanding that fitness tracking is the primary purchase driver, with other features appreciated only after ownership
The Apple Watch branding cleanup — eliminating 'Sport,' adding Nike and Hermès bookends, replacing gold Edition with ceramic — shows maturation of the product line
Apple strategically never said 'Bluetooth' during the AirPods presentation because the word carries negative associations with bad pairing and flaky connections
money_quotes: They could make a fortune with the Candy Crush IAP model, but Nintendo is smart enough to know that the money they're leaving on the table is an investment in their stellar brand.
If you think Apple Watch is a flop you are a moron.
The platform is like a pencil sketch turning into an inked illustration.
If you have to have a bump, own the bump, I say. And the new iPhones own their bumps.
It's like being a car pundit and judging the new Porsche 911 with a 'meh' because it looks like the previous 911, and never even considering what it's like to actually drive the new car.
'Bluetooth' is such a dirty word — so tied up with onerous pairing processes and flaky connections — that Phil Schiller never once said the word on stage.
When we look back decades from now, I think we'll see Myriad as Apple's Jobs-era typeface, and San Francisco as their Cook-era typeface.
key_insight: The iPhone 7 event marked Apple's maturing product strategy across hardware lines — from Nintendo's premium gaming validation to Apple Watch's fitness pivot to the iPhone's substance-over-style improvements — showing a company that prioritizes meaningful iteration over cosmetic reinvention.
